name: Castillo del Condestable Dávalos y piscina natural El Pelayos — circular desde Arenas de San Pedro
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Arenas De San Pedro are set within the province of Ávila, at the base of the Sierra de Gredos mountain range. This region features a varied terrain, including the granite massifs of the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park with its high passes and the contrasting, flatter Tiétar Valley. Extensive woodlands of pine, oak, and gall oak characterize the lower elevations, transitioning to bush vegetation at higher altitudes. Best touring cycling routes around Arenas De San Pedro The most popular touring cycling route is Castillo del Condestable Dávalos y piscina natural El Pelayos — circular desde Arenas de San Pedro, a 12.6 miles (20.3 km) trail that takes 1 hour 29 minutes to complete. This moderate route passes by a historic castle and a natural swimming area. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge – Forest Section loop from Arenas de San Pedro, a difficult 33.4 miles (53.8 km) path. This route features a medieval bridge and extends through forested sections of the landscape. Local touring cyclists also love the Ramacastañas – Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge loop from Inca 2.0, a 25.6 miles (41.3 km) trail leading through the area around Ramacastañas and past the Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge, often completed in about 3 hours 0 minutes. Touring cycling around Arenas De San Pedro is defined by the granite peaks of the Sierra de Gredos, the expansive Tiétar Valley, and dense woodlands. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging ascents. The routes in Arenas De San Pedro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from more than 4 reviews. More than 400 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Arenas De San Pedro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Arenas De San Pedro?
A: There are over 35 touring cycling routes around Arenas De San Pedro, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Arenas De San Pedro?
A: The terrain is quite varied, ranging from the granite massifs and high passes of the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park to the contrasting, flatter Tiétar Valley. You'll encounter extensive woodlands of pine, oak, and gall oak at lower elevations, transitioning to bush vegetation at higher altitudes. While mostly paved, some routes may include unpaved segments.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly touring cycling routes in the area?
A: While many routes are moderate to difficult, some sections of the Vía Verde del Alberche offer a relatively flat and accessible option, suitable for beginners and families. However, some parts might require careful navigation due to signage or shared road use. The route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1105086738/castillo-del-condestable-davalos-y-piscina-natural-el-pelayos-circular-desde-arenas-de-san-pedro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo del Condestable Dávalos y piscina natural El Pelayos — circular desde Arenas de San Pedro&lt;/a&gt; is rated as moderate and could be a good starting point.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, routes that venture into the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park, especially those around the Pozo de las Paredes and Mirador del Circo de Gredos, offer significant elevation gains. The area's high passes are frequently featured in the Vuelta a España. Consider routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17316232" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge – Forest Section loop from Arenas de San Pedro&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10372378" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge – Puerto de Pedro Bernardo loop from Arenas de San Pedro&lt;/a&gt; for demanding ascents.

Q: Can I find circular touring cycling routes around Arenas De San Pedro?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in the area are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1105086738/castillo-del-condestable-davalos-y-piscina-natural-el-pelayos-circular-desde-arenas-de-san-pedro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo del Condestable Dávalos y piscina natural El Pelayos — circular desde Arenas de San Pedro&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular option. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31685555" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ramacastañas – Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge loop from Inca 2.0&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Cycling routes often pass by significant natural features. You can explore the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park with its glacial lagoons and cirques, and the impressive Almanzor Peak. The Tiétar Valley offers contrasting landscapes. Specific natural highlights include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4095576" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Morezón Peak&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/540292" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Candeleda&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5754260" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo de las Paredes&lt;/a&gt;. The Águila Caves, just six kilometers from Arenas de San Pedro, are also a unique natural attraction.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical sights accessible by touring bike?
A: Absolutely. Many routes incorporate visits to historical sites. You can cycle past the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1105086738/castillo-del-condestable-davalos-y-piscina-natural-el-pelayos-circular-desde-arenas-de-san-pedro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo del Condestable Dávalos&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17316232" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, and the Don Álvaro de Luna castle. The town of Arenas de San Pedro itself features the Gothic church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción and the San Pedro de Alcántara sanctuary.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Arenas De San Pedro?
A: March is a popular month for cycling in the region. The yellow mountain broom blooms profusely in June, adding a distinctive visual appeal to the Gredos hillsides. While the Sierra de Gredos can be challenging in winter, the lower elevations and the Tiétar Valley offer more temperate conditions for cycling year-round.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling options in Arenas De San Pedro?
A: Yes, some parts of the Vía Verde del Alberche are suitable for families due to their relatively flat terrain. When planning a family outing, look for routes described as 'easy' or 'moderate' and check the elevation profiles to ensure they match your family's fitness level.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Arenas De San Pedro?
A: The touring cycling routes in Arenas De San Pedro are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from more than 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from challenging mountain passes to scenic valley rides, and the rich natural and historical attractions along the way.

Q: Are there opportunities to see wildlife while cycling in the region?
A: Yes, the Sierra de Gredos Regional Park is home to diverse plant and animal species, most notably the Iberian ibex. Cycling through the extensive woodlands of pine, oak, and gall oak also offers chances to spot various birds and other local fauna, especially during quieter times of the day.

Q: Are there any huts or shelters along the routes in case of bad weather or for overnight stays?
A: Yes, particularly in the Sierra de Gredos area, there are several huts and shelters. Notable options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2641568" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Elola Refuge – Laguna Grande de Gredos&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839762" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Victory Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4744395" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Grande Refuge&lt;/a&gt;. These can be useful for longer tours or as emergency shelters.
tips:
Castle built in the 15th century. In the year 1393, King Henry III granted the title of Villa to Arenas de San Pedro, handing over its lordship to Ruy López Dávalos, constable of Castile, who began its construction.
It is known as the Castle of the Sad Countess, because after the execution of Don Álvaro de Luna in 1453, Juan II allowed the town to remain in the possession of his widow, Doña Juana de Pimentel, popularly known by this nickname.

The Achecabos Bridge is commonly called a Roman bridge (since it was built on a previous bridge, predictably Roman). It is the oldest bridge in Arenas de San Pedro. It is expected that it was built in the eleventh century, so it is of medieval origin. It was widely used by travelers and transhumant shepherds, previously paying the toll or barge.
It consists of three arches, the central one being the largest of all. Its firm has been restored with cobblestone imitating the original. On both sides there is a one meter high stone parapet and various parts of its layout have been repaired with cement.
An ideal place to cool off on summer days. This natural area is located next to the road and is really well preserved. There you can have a drink as it has a bar and tables to relax.

waypoints.name: Castle of the Constable Dávalos (Castle of the Sad Countess)
